notes: Intel product introductions now up to 450MHz in speed.
IS executives captivated by sub-$1,000 PC siren song for corporate buying.
Many organizations in 1998 lagging behind Intel new product introductions.
Three years ago a new desktop PC would typically run at 150 MHz.
Generous 32MB of main memory was standard three years ago.
PC cost in volume was over $2,000 per unit in 1995.
Performance quite acceptable with 32MB but barely adequate with 16MB.
233 MHz Pentium II with 32MB becoming just adequate; everything older struggling.
Virtual memory usage on author's own PC about 60 MB.
32 MB now too little given 60 MB virtual memory usage observed.
Aberdeen's own PC planning demands a 3-5 year asset life.
Windows NT 5.0 upgrade expected in 2000. Actually released as Windows 2000 in Feb 2000.
Office 2000 expected in 1999. Actually released June 1999.
Internet Explorer 5.0 expected in 1999. Actually released March 1999.
Recommended 400 MHz Pentium II for knowledge workers.
Recommended 128 MB of memory for knowledge worker desktops.
Recommended 17-inch monitor for knowledge worker desktops.
Recommended configuration costs between $1,800 and $2,000 in late 1998.
Author notes recommended config will cost less in three months.
Microsoft-Intel 1999 PC Standard calls for minimum 300 MHz CPU.
Microsoft-Intel 1999 PC Standard calls for minimum 32 MB of memory.
Home computers selling for around $1,000 in volume.
Detailed software stack running on Aberdeen Group desktops in 1998.
Intranet, Internet, Extranet support including background updates consuming MIPS.
Tidal wave of electronic mail consuming resources.
Numerous shared-access network connections to workgroup data and applications.
Desktop systems management capability consuming background resources.
Kastner considered Celeron-class PCs but rejected them as false economy.
Celeron brand continues as Intel budget line. Sub-$1000 PCs became fully viable for corporate use.
Pentium II replaced by Pentium III (1999), Pentium 4, Core series. Intel remains x86 market leader.
Windows 95/98 succeeded by Windows 2000, XP, Vista, 7, 8, 10, 11.
Aberdeen Group acquired by Harte-Hanks 2008, later Spiceworks Ziff Davis.
Moore's Law eventually made sub-$1,000 PCs powerful enough for corporate use, invalidating Kastner's core premise.
Prediction that software demands would continue growing. Proved correct.
NetBEUI removed from Windows Vista onward. TCP/IP became sole standard.
10 Mbps Ethernet succeeded by 100Mbps, 1Gbps, 10Gbps.
Aberdeen's welded-case model: buy more than needed, avoid hardware upgrades. Anticipated sealed device trend.
Sealed non-upgradeable devices became standard (MacBook, Surface, ultrabooks) but for different reasons than Kastner predicted.
Rhetorical reference: have you tried a 486 PC with today's software lately?
Buyers seeing twice the CPU clock speed of a couple of years ago.
Buyers seeing twice the memory of a couple of years ago at bargain prices.
